---
layout: post
title: "Welcome to my blog"
date: 2013-09-28
---

<div>
<img src="data:image/jpeg;base64," width="219"/></div>
<div>
<div class="separator">
<a href="https://cdn.makeawebsitehub.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/04/social_media.jpg" imageanchor="1"><img border="0" data-original-height="534" data-original-width="800" height="213" src="https://cdn.makeawebsitehub.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/04/social_media.jpg" width="320"/></a></div>
<br/>
Inspired by the bloggers at the recent Social Media for Researchers course I went on at the University of Birmingham this is the platform I intend to use for my musings about education, technology and life in general...</div>

<div>
Useful things I learnt today were:</div>
<ol>
<li><div>
<a href="http://tweetdeck.com/" shape="rect" target="_blank">Tweetdeck</a> is a powerful way of managing your Twitter and Facebook feeds BUT the IoS version is soon going offline so I've opted for HootSuite instead.</div>
</li>
<li><div>
There is a whole new world of research out there ripe for the picking: netnography, mining of meta-data etc.</div>
</li>
<li><div>
<a href="http://analytics.topsy.com/" shape="rect" target="_blank">Topsy</a> and <a href="http://www.discovertext.com/" shape="rect" target="_blank">discovertext</a> are two excellent pieces of software for analysis social networks. They complement <a href="http://www.tweetstats.com//" shape="rect" target="_blank">TweetStats</a> and <a href="http://nodexl.codeplex.com/" shape="rect" target="_blank">NodeXL</a> which I had experimented with previously.</div>
</li>
</ol>
<br/>
<div>
The upshot of all this for my own research is that netnography and data-mining must form a significant part, if not all, of my collection of data about the use of social media platforms by school leaders.</div>
